methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $30M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Captura, a Series A climate and energy technology company founded by Harry Atwater and CX Xiang, is evaluated against a peer universe of 9 private companies and 1 public comparable (LNZA). The three most relevant same-stage, same-sector peers—Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M)—provide a direct market reference frame.

Two valuation methods are applicable. The stage-progression approach, using Captura's $30M+ total raised as a proxy base value and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement, yields $60M. The peer-median method anchors on the $150M median across the three comparable companies, with the peer range spanning $110M to $200M. This method directly reflects market pricing for comparable maturity and sector positioning.

Triangulating these two approaches produces a valuation range of $51M (low) to $172M (high), with a median estimate of $105M. The 3.4x spread between low and high reflects meaningful uncertainty regarding whether Captura's technology trajectory and capital efficiency align more closely with the lower quartile of peers (suggesting $51M–$80M) or the median-to-upper range ($105M–$172M). Key uncertainties include relative progress on commercialization milestones, capital efficiency relative to peer burn rates, and the durability of addressable market assumptions in the climate tech sector. The peer-median method carries greater weight given direct market comparability.
